        sd.longest_inactive_period,
        ai.num_ads_7d,
        ai.percentage_ad,
        ai.num_ads_clicked_7d,
        ai.num_ads_skipped_7d,
        ai.ad_engagement_score,
        tf.num_thumbs_up_7d,
        tf.num_thumbs_down_7d,
        tf.thumbs_feedback_score,
        pa.num_add_to_playlist,
        pa.num_remove_from_playlist,
        pa.num_playlists_created,
        pa.num_playlists_shared,
        pa.total_songs_in_playlists,
        sh.num_upgrades,
        sh.num_downgrades,
        sh.current_plan,
        sh.total_plan_changes,
        el.num_error_7d,
        el.error_severity,
        ue.num_events,
        ue.num_songs,
        ue.total_time_spent,
        ue.engagement_score,
        ue.engagement_trend,
        uf.num_add_friend,
        uf.total_friends_interacted,
        uf.total_messages_sent,
        ae.num_ads,
        ae.num_ads_clicked,
        ae.num_ads_skipped,
        ae.ad_experience_score,
        up.profile_completeness,
        pr.engagement_trend_score
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
    LEFT JOIN session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
    LEFT JOIN ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
    LEFT JOIN thumbs_feedback tf ON u.user_id = tf.user_id
    LEFT JOIN playlist_actions pa ON u.user_id = pa.user_id
    LEFT JOIN subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
    LEFT JOIN error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
    LEFT JOIN user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
    LEFT JOIN user_friends uf ON u.user_id = uf.user_id
    LEFT JOIN ad_experience ae ON u.user_id = ae.user_id
    LEFT JOIN user_profiles up ON u.user_id = up.user_id
    LEFT JOIN performance_reports pr ON u.user_id = pr.user_id
),
EngagementAverages AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(average_events_weekend) AS avg_weekend_events,
        AVG(average_events_weekday) AS avg_weekday_events,
        AVG(num_songs_played_7d) AS avg_songs_7d,
        AVG(num_songs_played_30d) AS avg_songs_30d,
        AVG(num_songs_played_90d) AS avg_songs_90d,
        AVG(total_events_7d) AS avg_total_events_7d,
        AVG(total_events_30d) AS avg_total_events_30d,
        AVG(total_events_90d) AS avg_total_events_90d,
        AVG(avg_time_per_session) AS avg_session_duration,
        AVG(num_ads_clicked_7d) AS avg_ads_clicked_7d,
        AVG(num_thumbs_up_7d) AS avg_thumbs_up_7d,
        AVG(num_thumbs_down_7d) AS avg_thumbs_down_7d,
        AVG(num_add_to_playlist) AS avg_add_playlist,
        AVG(num_remove_from_playlist) AS avg_remove_playlist,
        AVG(num_playlists_created) AS avg_playlists_created,
        AVG(num_playlists_shared) AS avg_playlists_shared,
        AVG(total_songs_in_playlists) AS avg_total_songs_playlists,
        AVG(num_upgrades) AS avg_num_upgrades,
        AVG(num_downgrades) AS avg_num_downgrades,
        AVG(total_plan_changes) AS avg_total_plan_changes,
        AVG(num_error_7d) AS avg_num_errors_7d,
        AVG(num_add_friend) AS avg_num_add_friend,
        AVG(total_friends_interacted) AS avg_total_friends_interacted,
        AVG(total_messages_sent) AS avg_total_messages_sent,
        AVG(ad_experience_score) AS avg_ad_experience_score,
        AVG(profile_completeness) AS avg_profile_completeness,
        AVG(engagement_trend_score) AS avg_engagement_trend_score
    FROM UserEngagementSummary
),
UserEngagementScores AS (
    SELECT 
        ues.user_id,
        ues.user_churned,
        ues.average_events_weekend,
        ues.average_events_weekday,
        ues.num_songs_played_7d,
        ues.num_songs_played_30d,
        ues.num_songs_played_90d,
        ues.total_events_7d,
        ues.total_events_30d,
        ues.total_events_90d,
        ues.avg_time_per_session,
        ues.num_ads_clicked_7d,
        ues.num_thumbs_up_7d,
        ues.num_thumbs_down_7d,
        ues.num_add_to_playlist,
        ues.num_remove_from_playlist,
        ues.num_playlists_created,
        ues.num_playlists_shared,
        ues.total_songs_in_playlists,
        ues.num_upgrades,
        ues.num_downgrades,
        ues.total_plan_changes,
        ues.num_error_7d,
        ues.ad_experience_score,
        ues.thumbs_feedback_score,
        ues.engagement_score,
        ea.avg_weekend_events,
        ea.avg_weekday_events,
        ea.avg_songs_7d,
        ea.avg_songs_30d,
        ea.avg_songs_90d,
        ea.avg_total_events_7d,
        ea.avg_total_events_30d,
        ea.avg_total_events_90d,
        ea.avg_session_duration,
        ea.avg_ads_clicked_7d,
        ea.avg_thumbs_up_7d,
        ea.avg_thumbs_down_7d,
        ea.avg_add_playlist,
        ea.avg_remove_playlist,
        ea.avg_playlists_created,
        ea.avg_playlists_shared,
        ea.avg_total_songs_playlists,
        ea.avg_num_upgrades,
        ea.avg_num_downgrades,
        ea.avg_total_plan_changes,
        ea.avg_num_errors_7d,
        ea.avg_num_add_friend,
        ea.avg_total_friends_interacted,
        ea.avg_total_messages_sent,
        ea.avg_ad_experience_score,
        ea.avg_profile_completeness,
        ea.avg_engagement_trend_score,
        (CASE 
            WHEN ues.average_events_weekend < ea.avg_weekend_events THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.average_events_weekday < ea.avg_weekday_events THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_songs_played_7d < ea.avg_songs_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_songs_played_30d < ea.avg_songs_30d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_songs_played_90d < ea.avg_songs_90d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.total_events_7d < ea.avg_total_events_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.total_events_30d < ea.avg_total_events_30d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.total_events_90d < ea.avg_total_events_90d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.avg_time_per_session < ea.avg_session_duration THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_ads_clicked_7d < ea.avg_ads_clicked_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_thumbs_up_7d < ea.avg_thumbs_up_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_thumbs_down_7d < ea.avg_thumbs_down_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_add_to_playlist < ea.avg_add_playlist THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_remove_from_playlist < ea.avg_remove_playlist THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_playlists_created < ea.avg_playlists_created THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_playlists_shared < ea.avg_playlists_shared THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.total_songs_in_playlists < ea.avg_total_songs_playlists THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_upgrades < ea.avg_num_upgrades THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_downgrades < ea.avg_num_downgrades THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.total_plan_changes < ea.avg_total_plan_changes THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.num_error_7d < ea.avg_num_errors_7d THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.ad_experience_score < ea.avg_ad_experience_score THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.thumbs_feedback_score < ea.avg_profile_completeness THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END +
         CASE 
            WHEN ues.engagement_score < ea.avg_engagement_trend_score THEN 1 ELSE 0 
         END) AS engagement_below_avg_count
    FROM UserEngagementSummary ues
    CROSS JOIN EngagementAverages ea
)
SELECT 
    user_id
FROM UserEngagementScores
WHERE user_churned = 0
ORDER BY engagement_below_avg_count DESC
LIMIT 10;

question: I want to know the IDs of users who have a decreasing engagement trend, as indicated by an engagement trend score less than 0.5, and have experienced more than 5 major or critical errors in the last 7 days, ordered by their engagement trend score from lowest to highest and then by the number of errors from highest to lowest.
cot:
To translate the provided natural language question into an efficient SQL query, we need to break down the requirements and identify the relevant tables and columns to retrieve the required data. Here is the step-by-step reasoning:

### Step 1: Identify the relevant tables and columns

1. **Users with decreasing engagement trend**:
   - This information can be found in the `performance_reports` table with the columns `user_id` and `engagement_trend_score`.
   - We need users with `engagement_trend_score < 0.5`.

2. **Users who experienced more than 5 major or critical errors in the last 7 days**:
   - This information is available in the `error_logs` table with the columns `user_id`, `num_error_7d`, and `error_severity`.
   - We need users with `num_error_7d > 5` and `error_severity IN ('major', 'critical')`.

### Step 2: Combine the data from both tables

- To get users who meet both criteria, we will perform an `INNER JOIN` between `performance_reports` and `error_logs` on the `user_id` column.
- We will filter the data based on the conditions specified for engagement trend score and error severity.

### Step 3: Order the results

- The results should be ordered by `engagement_trend_score` in ascending order (lowest to highest).
- If there are ties in the engagement trend score, we will further order them by `num_error_7d` in descending order (highest to lowest).

### Step 4: Select only the required columns

- The question only asks for the `user_id` of the users who meet the criteria, so we will select only this column.

### Step 5: Write the SQL query

Based on the above steps, here is the SQL query:

```sql
SELECT 
    pr.user_id
FROM 
    performance_reports pr
INNER JOIN 
    error_logs el ON pr.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    pr.engagement_trend_score < 0.5
    AND el.num_error_7d > 5
    AND el.error_severity IN ('major', 'critical')
ORDER BY 
    pr.engagement_trend_score ASC, 
    el.num_error_7d DESC;
```

### Explanation

- **INNER JOIN**: Ensures that we only consider users who have both engagement trend data and error data.
- **WHERE clause**: Applies the necessary filters to get users with a decreasing engagement trend and more than 5 major or critical errors in the last 7 days.
- **ORDER BY**: Sorts the results as specified, first by engagement trend score in ascending order and then by the number of errors in descending order for ties in the trend score.
sql:
SELECT 
    pr.user_id
FROM 
    performance_reports pr
INNER JOIN 
    error_logs el ON pr.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    pr.engagement_trend_score < 0.5
    AND el.num_error_7d > 5
    AND el.error_severity IN ('major', 'critical')
ORDER BY 
    pr.engagement_trend_score ASC, 
    el.num_error_7d DESC;

question: I want to know the IDs of users with a high risk of churning (a risk score greater than 0.7), ordered from highest to lowest risk, considering factors such as their engagement levels, ad interactions, session data, subscription plans, and time since last active.
cot:
To translate the given natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to determine the relevant tables and columns that capture the factors mentioned in the question: engagement levels, ad interactions, session data, subscription plans, and time since last active. We will then construct a query that calculates a churn risk score for each user based on these factors and filters out those users with a risk score greater than 0.7. The results will be ordered from highest to lowest risk.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

1. **Identify Relevant Tables and Columns**:
   - **users**: `user_id`, `user_churned`, `days_since_active`, `repeats_ratio`, `subscription_plan`
   - **user_engagement**: `engagement_score` (to gauge user engagement)
   - **ad_interactions**: `ad_engagement_score` (to measure ad interactions)
   - **session_data**: `longest_inactive_period` (to assess session data)
   
2. **Determine Factors for Churn Risk Score**:
   - `engagement_score` from `user_engagement`: Higher scores indicate better engagement, which should ideally lower churn risk.
   - `ad_engagement_score` from `ad_interactions`: Higher scores indicate better ad interaction, which could also reduce churn risk.
   - `days_since_active` from `users`: Longer inactivity increases churn risk.
   - `repeats_ratio` from `users`: Higher ratio indicates more frequent use, which should reduce churn risk.
   - `longest_inactive_period` from `session_data`: Longer periods of inactivity increase churn risk.
   - `subscription_plan` from `users`: Users on free plans might have higher churn risk.

3. **Formulate Churn Risk Score**:
   - Use a weighted average to calculate the churn risk score:
     - Engagement score: Higher engagement score should reduce churn risk.
     - Ad engagement score: Higher ad engagement score should reduce churn risk.
     - Days since active: More days since last active should increase churn risk.
     - Repeats ratio: Higher repeats ratio should reduce churn risk.
     - Longest inactive period: Longer inactivity should increase churn risk.
     - Subscription plan: Users on free plans should have a higher risk.

4. **Construct the SQL Query**:
   - Join the necessary tables on `user_id`.
   - Calculate the churn risk score.
   - Filter users with a churn risk score greater than 0.7.
   - Order the results by churn risk score in descending order.

```sql
SELECT 
    u.user_id
FROM 
    users u
JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
JOIN 
    ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
JOIN 
    session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
WHERE 
    0.3 * (1 - (ue.engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (1 - (ai.ad_engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (u.days_since_active / 365) +
    0.1 * (u.repeats_ratio) +
    0.1 * (sd.longest_inactive_period / 30) +
    0.1 * (CASE WHEN u.subscription_plan = 'Free' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0.7
ORDER BY 
    0.3 * (1 - (ue.engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (1 - (ai.ad_engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (u.days_since_active / 365) +
    0.1 * (u.repeats_ratio) +
    0.1 * (sd.longest_inactive_period / 30) +
    0.1 * (CASE WHEN u.subscription_plan = 'Free' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) DESC;
```

### Explanation:
- **Joins**: We join `users`, `user_engagement`, `ad_interactions`, and `session_data` tables on `user_id` to gather all necessary data for calculating the churn risk score.
- **Churn Risk Score Calculation**: We compute the churn risk score using a weighted formula. Each factor is adjusted to a scale between 0 and 1, and the weights sum to 1.
- **Filtering and Ordering**: We filter users with a churn risk score greater than 0.7 and order the results by the churn risk score in descending order to prioritize users with the highest risk.
sql:
SELECT 
    u.user_id
FROM 
    users u
JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
JOIN 
    ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
JOIN 
    session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
WHERE 
    0.3 * (1 - (ue.engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (1 - (ai.ad_engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (u.days_since_active / 365) +
    0.1 * (u.repeats_ratio) +
    0.1 * (sd.longest_inactive_period / 30) +
    0.1 * (CASE WHEN u.subscription_plan = 'Free' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0.7
ORDER BY 
    0.3 * (1 - (ue.engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (1 - (ai.ad_engagement_score / 100)) +
    0.2 * (u.days_since_active / 365) +
    0.1 * (u.repeats_ratio) +
    0.1 * (sd.longest_inactive_period / 30) +
    0.1 * (CASE WHEN u.subscription_plan = 'Free' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) DESC;

question:
**User**: I want to analyze user engagement and churn rates.
**Assistant**: What kind of users would you like to analyze?
**User**: Active users who have been inactive for more than 30 days.
**Assistant**: What specific information would you like to know about these users?
**User**: I'd like to know their user ID, churn status, days since active, repeats ratio, subscription plan, signup date, last active date, and various engagement metrics such as average events, songs played, session duration, and ad interactions.
**Assistant**: Would you like to filter out any users based on their subscription plan changes?
**User**: Yes, I'd like to exclude users who have changed their plan in the last 30 days.
**Assistant**: How would you like to classify the users in the analysis?
**User**: I'd like to classify them as either 'Churned' or 'Active' based on their churn status.
**Assistant**: What kind of output are you expecting from this analysis?
**User**: I'd like to see the count of users in each churn status group, ordered from highest to lowest.
**Assistant**: OK, I will help you translate your request into an SQL query.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to follow these steps:

1. **Identify the Relevant Tables and Columns**:
   - From the `users` table: `user_id`, `user_churned`, `days_since_active`, `repeats_ratio`, `subscription_plan`, `signup_date`, `last_active_date`.
   - From the `user_metrics` table: `average_events_weekend`, `average_events_weekday`, `num_songs_played_30d`, `total_events_30d`, `average_session_duration_weekend`, `average_session_duration_weekday`.
   - From the `session_data` table: `avg_time_per_session`, `avg_events_per_session`.
   - From the `ad_interactions` table: `num_ads_7d`, `num_ads_clicked_7d`, `num_ads_skipped_7d`, `ad_engagement_score`.
   - From the `subscription_history` table: `last_plan_change_date`.

2. **Filter Active Users**:
   - Users who have been inactive for more than 30 days: `days_since_active > 30`.

3. **Exclude Users with Recent Subscription Plan Changes**:
   - Exclude users who have changed their plan in the last 30 days: `last_plan_change_date < DATE('now', '-30 days')`.

4. **Classify Users based on Churn Status**:
   - Use `CASE` statement to classify users as 'Churned' or 'Active'.

5. **Count Users in Each Churn Status Group**:
   - Group by `churn_status` and order by the count of users in descending order.

### SQL Query

```sql
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN u.user_churned = 1 THEN 'Churned'
        ELSE 'Active'
    END AS churn_status,
    COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM 
    users u
LEFT JOIN 
    user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    ad_interactions ad ON u.user_id = ad.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
WHERE 
    u.days_since_active > 30
    AND (sh.last_plan_change_date IS NULL OR sh.last_plan_change_date < DATE('now', '-30 days'))
GROUP BY 
    churn_status
ORDER BY 
    user_count DESC;
```

### Explanation of the Query

- **LEFT JOIN**: We use `LEFT JOIN` to include all users from the `users` table and their corresponding data from the `user_metrics`, `session_data`, `ad_interactions`, and `subscription_history` tables. This ensures we have all the required engagement metrics and subscription history for each user.
- **WHERE Clause**: We filter the users who have been inactive for more than 30 days (`u.days_since_active > 30`) and exclude those who have changed their subscription plan in the last 30 days (`sh.last_plan_change_date < DATE('now', '-30 days')`).
- **CASE Statement**: We classify each user as 'Churned' or 'Active' based on the `user_churned` flag.
- **GROUP BY and ORDER BY**: We group the results by `churn_status` and order them by the count of users in descending order to see the highest counts at the top.

This query efficiently aggregates the necessary information to analyze user engagement and churn rates for the specified criteria.
sql:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN u.user_churned = 1 THEN 'Churned'
        ELSE 'Active'
    END AS churn_status,
    COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM 
    users u
LEFT JOIN 
    user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    ad_interactions ad ON u.user_id = ad.user_id
LEFT JOIN 
    subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
WHERE 
    u.days_since_active > 30
    AND (sh.last_plan_change_date IS NULL OR sh.last_plan_change_date < DATE('now', '-30 days'))
GROUP BY 
    churn_status
ORDER BY 
    user_count DESC;

question: Find the user IDs of those who are at a high risk of leaving the service, meaning they have been inactive for over 60 days, have a low engagement score, frequently skip ads, experience errors, and have downgraded their subscription recently.
external_knowledge: High-risk users are those who exhibit behaviors indicating they might stop using the service. Inactive users are those who haven't engaged with the service for an extended period (more than 60 days); low engagement scores signify minimal interaction; frequent ad skipping suggests dissatisfaction with the ad experience; recurring errors indicate poor service quality; and recent subscription downgrades imply dissatisfaction with the current service level.
cot:
To derive the SQL query for identifying high-risk users based on the specified criteria, we need to follow these steps:

1. **Identify Inactive Users**: Users who have been inactive for more than 60 days can be identified using the `days_since_active` column from the `users` table.
2. **Identify Users with Low Engagement Scores**: Users with an engagement score less than 0.5 can be identified using the `engagement_score` column from the `user_engagement` table.
3. **Identify Users Who Frequently Skip Ads**: Users who skip more than 10 ads in the last 7 days can be identified using the `num_ads_skipped` column from the `ad_experience` table.
4. **Identify Users with Recurring Errors**: Users who experience more than 5 errors in the last 7 days can be identified using the `num_error_7d` column from the `error_logs` table.
5. **Identify Users Who Recently Downgraded Their Subscription**: Users who have downgraded their subscription in the last 30 days can be identified using the `num_downgrades` and `last_plan_change_date` columns from the `subscription_history` table.

Each of these criteria can be encapsulated in a Common Table Expression (CTE) for clarity and reusability. The final CTE `HighRiskUsers` will join all these CTEs to find users who meet all the high-risk criteria.

### Step-by-Step SQL Query Derivation

1. **User Inactivity**:
   ```sql
   WITH UserInactivity AS (
       SELECT user_id
       FROM users
       WHERE days_since_active > 60
   )
   ```

2. **User Engagement**:
   ```sql
   UserEngagement AS (
       SELECT user_id
       FROM user_engagement
       WHERE engagement_score < 0.5
   )
   ```

3. **Ad Experience**:
   ```sql
   AdExperience AS (
       SELECT user_id
       FROM ad_experience
       WHERE num_ads_skipped > 10
   )
   ```

4. **User Errors**:
   ```sql
   UserErrors AS (
       SELECT user_id
       FROM error_logs
       WHERE num_error_7d > 5
   )
   ```

5. **Subscription Downgrades**:
   ```sql
   SubscriptionDowngrades AS (
       SELECT user_id
       FROM subscription_history
       WHERE num_downgrades > 0 AND last_plan_change_date >= DATE('now', '-30 days')
   )
   ```

6. **High-Risk Users**:
   ```sql
   HighRiskUsers AS (
       SELECT ui.user_id
       FROM UserInactivity ui
       JOIN UserEngagement ue ON ui.user_id = ue.user_id
       JOIN AdExperience ae ON ui.user_id = ae.user_id
       JOIN UserErrors ue2 ON ui.user_id = ue2.user_id
       JOIN SubscriptionDowngrades sd ON ui.user_id = sd.user_id
   )
   ```

7. **Final Selection**:
   ```sql
   SELECT user_id
   FROM HighRiskUsers;
   ```

### Final SQL Query

Combining all the steps into a single SQL query:

```sql
WITH UserInactivity AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM users
    WHERE days_since_active > 60
),
UserEngagement AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM user_engagement
    WHERE engagement_score < 0.5
),
AdExperience AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM ad_experience
    WHERE num_ads_skipped > 10
),
UserErrors AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM error_logs
    WHERE num_error_7d > 5
),
SubscriptionDowngrades AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM subscription_history
    WHERE num_downgrades > 0 AND last_plan_change_date >= DATE('now', '-30 days')
),
HighRiskUsers AS (
    SELECT ui.user_id
    FROM UserInactivity ui
    JOIN UserEngagement ue ON ui.user_id = ue.user_id
    JOIN AdExperience ae ON ui.user_id = ae.user_id
    JOIN UserErrors ue2 ON ui.user_id = ue2.user_id
    JOIN SubscriptionDowngrades sd ON ui.user_id = sd.user_id
)
SELECT user_id
FROM HighRiskUsers;
```

This query effectively identifies high-risk users by joining multiple CTEs that each filter users based on specific high-risk criteria.
sql:
WITH UserInactivity AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM users
    WHERE days_since_active > 60
),
UserEngagement AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM user_engagement
    WHERE engagement_score < 0.5
),
AdExperience AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM ad_experience
    WHERE num_ads_skipped > 10
),
UserErrors AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM error_logs
    WHERE num_error_7d > 5
),
SubscriptionDowngrades AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM subscription_history
    WHERE num_downgrades > 0 AND last_plan_change_date >= DATE('now', '-30 days')
),
HighRiskUsers AS (
    SELECT ui.user_id
    FROM UserInactivity ui
    JOIN UserEngagement ue ON ui.user_id = ue.user_id
    JOIN AdExperience ae ON ui.user_id = ae.user_id
    JOIN UserErrors ue2 ON ui.user_id = ue2.user_id
    JOIN SubscriptionDowngrades sd ON ui.user_id = sd.user_id
)
SELECT user_id
FROM HighRiskUsers;

question: What is the average engagement score of all users, adjusted for churn status and ad experience?
cot:
To derive the SQL query for the average engagement score adjusted for churn status and ad experience, we'll follow these steps:

1. **Identify Relevant Tables**: 
   - `users`: Contains user information including `user_churned`.
   - `user_engagement`: Contains the `engagement_score` for each user.
   - `ad_experience`: Contains the `ad_experience_score` for each user.

2. **Understand the Adjustments**:
   - **Churn Adjustment**: If a user has churned (`user_churned = 1`), their engagement score should be reduced by 10%.
   - **Ad Experience Adjustment**: If a user has a high ad experience score (`ad_experience_score > 0.8`), their engagement score should be increased by 10%.

3. **Join Relevant Tables**:
   - Join `users` with `user_engagement` on `user_id` to get the engagement score.
   - Join `ad_experience` with the result on `user_id` to get the ad experience score.

4. **Apply Adjustments**:
   - Use a `CASE` statement to apply the churn adjustment factor.
   - Use another `CASE` statement to apply the ad experience adjustment factor.

5. **Calculate the Average**:
   - Calculate the average of the adjusted engagement scores.

Here is the step-by-step SQL query derivation:

```sql
-- Step 1: Select the necessary columns from users and user_engagement
WITH UserEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        u.user_id,
        u.user_churned,
        ue.engagement_score
    FROM 
        users u
    JOIN 
        user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
),

-- Step 2: Apply the churn adjustment to the engagement score
ChurnAdjustedEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        engagement_score * (1 - 0.1 * (CASE WHEN user_churned = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)) AS churn_adjusted_engagement_score
    FROM 
        UserEngagement
),

-- Step 3: Apply the ad experience adjustment to the churn-adjusted engagement score
AdExperienceAdjustedEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        cae.user_id,
        cae.churn_adjusted_engagement_score * (1 + 0.1 * (CASE WHEN ae.ad_experience_score > 0.8 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)) AS final_engagement_score
    FROM 
        ChurnAdjustedEngagement cae
    LEFT JOIN 
        ad_experience ae ON cae.user_id = ae.user_id
)

-- Step 4: Calculate the average of the final engagement scores
SELECT 
    AVG(final_engagement_score) AS average_final_engagement_score
FROM 
    AdExperienceAdjustedEngagement;
```

### Explanation:
- **UserEngagement CTE**: We first select the necessary columns from the `users` and `user_engagement` tables.
- **ChurnAdjustedEngagement CTE**: We adjust the engagement score by reducing it by 10% for churned users.
- **AdExperienceAdjustedEngagement CTE**: We further adjust the score by increasing it by 10% for users with a high ad experience score.
- **Final SELECT**: We calculate the average of the final adjusted engagement scores to get the desired result.

This query efficiently combines the necessary data and applies the required adjustments to provide the average engagement score considering churn and ad experience.
sql:
-- Step 1: Select the necessary columns from users and user_engagement
WITH UserEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        u.user_id,
        u.user_churned,
        ue.engagement_score
    FROM 
        users u
    JOIN 
        user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
),

-- Step 2: Apply the churn adjustment to the engagement score
ChurnAdjustedEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        engagement_score * (1 - 0.1 * (CASE WHEN user_churned = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)) AS churn_adjusted_engagement_score
    FROM 
        UserEngagement
),

-- Step 3: Apply the ad experience adjustment to the churn-adjusted engagement score
AdExperienceAdjustedEngagement AS (
    SELECT 
        cae.user_id,
        cae.churn_adjusted_engagement_score * (1 + 0.1 * (CASE WHEN ae.ad_experience_score > 0.8 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)) AS final_engagement_score
    FROM 
        ChurnAdjustedEngagement cae
    LEFT JOIN 
        ad_experience ae ON cae.user_id = ae.user_id
)

-- Step 4: Calculate the average of the final engagement scores
SELECT 
    AVG(final_engagement_score) AS average_final_engagement_score
FROM 
    AdExperienceAdjustedEngagement;

question: Hey, could you help me find the IDs of the top 10 users with the highest engagement scores above 0.85 and who have had more than 3 errors in the last 7 days?
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to identify the necessary tables and columns required to answer the question. The question asks for the IDs of the top 10 users who meet two specific criteria:

1. Their engagement score is above 0.85.
2. They have had more than 3 errors in the last 7 days.

We can achieve this by joining the `users` table with the `user_engagement` table to filter users based on their engagement score, and then joining the result with the `error_logs` table to filter users based on the number of errors in the last 7 days. Since the query needs to return the top 10 users based on the engagement score, we will use the `ORDER BY` clause along with `LIMIT` to get the top 10 results.

Here's the step-by-step breakdown:

1. **Join `users` and `user_engagement` tables**:
   - We need to join these tables to access the engagement score of each user.

2. **Join the result with `error_logs`**:
   - This join will allow us to filter users based on the number of errors they have had in the last 7 days.

3. **Filter users with engagement score above 0.85**:
   - Use a `WHERE` clause to include only users with an engagement score greater than 0.85.

4. **Filter users with more than 3 errors in the last 7 days**:
   - Continue using the `WHERE` clause to include only users with more than 3 errors in the last 7 days.

5. **Order by engagement score and limit to top 10**:
   - Use `ORDER BY` to sort users by their engagement score in descending order and `LIMIT` to get the top 10 users.

Here is the final SQL query:

```sql
SELECT 
    u.user_id
FROM 
    users u
JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
JOIN 
    error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    ue.engagement_score > 0.85
    AND el.num_error_7d > 3
ORDER BY 
    ue.engagement_score DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

This query efficiently combines the necessary data from the `users`, `user_engagement`, and `error_logs` tables to filter and sort users based on the given criteria, ensuring that only the top 10 users with the highest engagement scores above 0.85 and more than 3 errors in the last 7 days are returned.
sql:
SELECT 
    u.user_id
FROM 
    users u
JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
JOIN 
    error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    ue.engagement_score > 0.85
    AND el.num_error_7d > 3
ORDER BY 
    ue.engagement_score DESC
LIMIT 10;

question: Find the email addresses of the top 10 highly engaged users who have experienced a large number of errors in the last 7 days, ranking them by their engagement level and error frequency.
external_knowledge: Highly engaged users are those with an engagement score > 0.8; a high engagement score indicates users who frequently interact with the platform; error-prone users are those with > 5 errors logged in the last 7 days; the number of errors logged in the last 7 days indicates the frequency of errors experienced by the user.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an SQL query, we need to identify the key components and perform the necessary joins and filtering based on the provided schema and question requirements. Here's a step-by-step breakdown:

### Step 1: Define the Criteria for Highly Engaged Users
- A highly engaged user is defined as someone with an `engagement_score` greater than 0.8.

### Step 2: Define the Criteria for Error-Prone Users
- An error-prone user is defined as someone with more than 5 errors logged in the last 7 days (`num_error_7d` > 5).

### Step 3: Join the Relevant Tables
- We need to join the `users` table with the `user_engagement` table to get the engagement scores.
- Similarly, we need to join the `users` table with the `error_logs` table to get the number of errors logged in the last 7 days.

### Step 4: Select the Required Columns
- We need to select the `email` addresses of the users who meet both criteria.

### Step 5: Apply the Filters
- Use the `WHERE` clause to filter users based on the engagement score and the number of errors.

### Step 6: Order the Results
- Order the results first by `engagement_score` in descending order and then by `num_error_7d` in descending order to get the users with the highest engagement and the most errors first.

### Step 7: Limit the Results
- Limit the results to the top 10 users.

Based on these steps, here is the SQL query:

```sql
SELECT 
    u.email
FROM 
    users u
INNER JOIN 
    user_engagement e ON u.user_id = e.user_id
INNER JOIN 
    error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    e.engagement_score > 0.8
    AND el.num_error_7d > 5
ORDER BY 
    e.engagement_score DESC, 
    el.num_error_7d DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

### Explanation:
- **INNER JOIN**: We use `INNER JOIN` to combine rows from the `users`, `user_engagement`, and `error_logs` tables where the `user_id` matches.
- **WHERE Clause**: Filters the results to include only those users who have an `engagement_score` greater than 0.8 and more than 5 errors in the last 7 days.
- **ORDER BY**: Sorts the results by `engagement_score` in descending order and then by `num_error_7d` in descending order.
- **LIMIT**: Restricts the output to the top 10 users based on the specified sorting criteria.
sql:
SELECT 
    u.email
FROM 
    users u
INNER JOIN 
    user_engagement e ON u.user_id = e.user_id
INNER JOIN 
    error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
    e.engagement_score > 0.8
    AND el.num_error_7d > 5
ORDER BY 
    e.engagement_score DESC, 
    el.num_error_7d DESC
LIMIT 10;

question: What are the user IDs of the top 10 users who have not churned but are at the highest risk of churning, based on their inactivity, low engagement, and other factors?
cot:
To determine the top 10 users who have not churned but are at the highest risk of churning, we need to consider several factors such as inactivity, low engagement, and other relevant metrics. The reference solution provided a good starting point, but we need to refine and simplify it to ensure it accurately captures the users at the highest risk.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

1. **Filter Users Who Have Not Churned:**
   We need to start with users who are still active (i.e., `user_churned = 0`).

2. **Join Relevant Tables:**
   We need to join tables that contain relevant metrics to assess the risk of churn:
   - `user_metrics` to get engagement metrics like `num_songs_played_7d`, `total_events_7d`, etc.
   - `session_data` to get session-related metrics like `total_session_duration`, `avg_time_per_session`, etc.
   - `ad_interactions` to get ad-related engagement metrics like `ad_engagement_score`.
   - `subscription_history` to get metrics related to subscription changes like `total_plan_changes`.

3. **Calculate a Churn Risk Score:**
   We can create a risk score based on a combination of inactivity (`days_since_active`), low engagement (`num_songs_played_7d`, `total_events_7d`), and other factors. We'll prioritize these factors by ordering by `days_since_active` in descending order and engagement metrics in ascending order.

4. **Rank Users Based on the Churn Risk Score:**
   We'll use the `ROW_NUMBER` window function to rank users according to our calculated risk factors.

5. **Select the Top 10 Users:**
   Finally, we'll select the top 10 users from the ranked list who have not churned.

### SQL Query:

```sql
WITH UserRiskFactors AS (
    SELECT 
        u.user_id,
        u.days_since_active,
        um.num_songs_played_7d,
        um.total_events_7d,
        s.total_session_duration,
        s.avg_time_per_session,
        ai.ad_engagement_score,
        sh.total_plan_changes
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
    LEFT JOIN session_data s ON u.user_id = s.user_id
    LEFT JOIN ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
    LEFT JOIN subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
    WHERE u.user_churned = 0
),
ChurnRiskRanking AS (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY 
            days_since_active DESC,
            num_songs_played_7d ASC,
            total_events_7d ASC,
            total_session_duration ASC,
            avg_time_per_session ASC,
            ad_engagement_score ASC,
            total_plan_changes ASC
        ) AS churn_risk_rank
    FROM UserRiskFactors
)
SELECT 
    user_id
FROM ChurnRiskRanking
WHERE churn_risk_rank <= 10;
```

### Explanation:

- **UserRiskFactors CTE:** Filters out users who have churned and selects relevant metrics from the joined tables.
- **ChurnRiskRanking CTE:** Assigns a rank to each user based on the defined risk factors.
- **Final SELECT:** Retrieves the top 10 users with the highest churn risk rank.

This query ensures that we focus only on the necessary metrics and correctly identify the users who are at the highest risk of churning.
sql:
WITH UserRiskFactors AS (
    SELECT 
        u.user_id,
        u.days_since_active,
        um.num_songs_played_7d,
        um.total_events_7d,
        s.total_session_duration,
        s.avg_time_per_session,
        ai.ad_engagement_score,
        sh.total_plan_changes
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
    LEFT JOIN session_data s ON u.user_id = s.user_id
    LEFT JOIN ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
    LEFT JOIN subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
    WHERE u.user_churned = 0
),
ChurnRiskRanking AS (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY 
            days_since_active DESC,
            num_songs_played_7d ASC,
            total_events_7d ASC,
            total_session_duration ASC,
            avg_time_per_session ASC,
            ad_engagement_score ASC,
            total_plan_changes ASC
        ) AS churn_risk_rank
    FROM UserRiskFactors
)
SELECT 
    user_id
FROM ChurnRiskRanking
WHERE churn_risk_rank <= 10;

question: Find the user IDs, email addresses, engagement scores, and ad experience scores of all active users who have an engagement score greater than 0.7 and an ad experience score less than 0.5, and return the results sorted by engagement score in descending order and ad experience score in ascending order.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an SQL query, let's break down the requirements and derive the necessary components of the SQL statement.

### Step-by-Step Breakdown:

1. **Identify the Tables and Columns Needed**:
   - **users**: To get `user_id` and `email`.
   - **user_engagement**: To get `engagement_score`.
   - **ad_experience**: To get `ad_experience_score`.

2. **Conditions**:
   - The user should be active (`user_churned = 0`).
   - The user's engagement score should be greater than 0.7.
   - The user's ad experience score should be less than 0.5.

3. **Sorting**:
   - Results should be sorted by `engagement_score` in descending order.
   - Within the same engagement score, results should be sorted by `ad_experience_score` in ascending order.

### SQL Query Construction:

1. **SELECT Clause**:
   - We need to select `user_id`, `email` from the `users` table.
   - We need to select `engagement_score` from the `user_engagement` table.
   - We need to select `ad_experience_score` from the `ad_experience` table.

2. **FROM Clause**:
   - Start with the `users` table as the base.

3. **JOIN Clauses**:
   - Join `user_engagement` on `user_id` to get the engagement score.
   - Join `ad_experience` on `user_id` to get the ad experience score.

4. **WHERE Clause**:
   - Apply the conditions: `user_churned = 0`, `engagement_score > 0.7`, and `ad_experience_score < 0.5`.

5. **ORDER BY Clause**:
   - Order by `engagement_score` in descending order.
   - Then, order by `ad_experience_score` in ascending order.

### Final SQL Query:

```sql
SELECT 
    u.user_id, 
    u.email,
    ue.engagement_score,
    ae.ad_experience_score
FROM 
    users u
INNER JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
INNER JOIN 
    ad_experience ae ON u.user_id = ae.user_id
WHERE 
    u.user_churned = 0
    AND ue.engagement_score > 0.7
    AND ae.ad_experience_score < 0.5
ORDER BY 
    ue.engagement_score DESC, 
    ae.ad_experience_score ASC;
```

This query ensures that we only get data for active users meeting the specified conditions and sorts the results as requested.
sql:
SELECT 
    u.user_id, 
    u.email,
    ue.engagement_score,
    ae.ad_experience_score
FROM 
    users u
INNER JOIN 
    user_engagement ue ON u.user_id = ue.user_id
INNER JOIN 
    ad_experience ae ON u.user_id = ae.user_id
WHERE 
    u.user_churned = 0
    AND ue.engagement_score > 0.7
    AND ae.ad_experience_score < 0.5
ORDER BY 
    ue.engagement_score DESC, 
    ae.ad_experience_score ASC;

question: What are the IDs of active users who have had many events and errors recently, and spent a significant amount of time in their sessions?
external_knowledge: 'many events' refers to an average of more than 100 events in the last 7 days; 'recently' refers to the last 7 days; 'significant amount of time' refers to an average session time of more than 5000 seconds (approximately 83 minutes); 'active users' refers to users who have not churned (i.e., left the service); 'errors' refers to errors logged by the user in the last 7 days.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an SQL query, we need to carefully identify the criteria specified and map them to the relevant tables and columns in the provided database schema.

**Step-by-Step Breakdown**:

1. **Identify the criteria for 'active users'**:
   - 'Active users' are those who have not churned, which translates to `user_churned = 0` in the `users` table.

2. **Identify the criteria for 'many events'**:
   - 'Many events' is defined as an average of more than 100 events in the last 7 days. This information is available in the `total_events_7d` column of the `user_metrics` table.

3. **Identify the criteria for 'significant amount of time'**:
   - 'Significant amount of time' refers to an average session time of more than 5000 seconds. This information is available in the `avg_time_per_session` column of the `session_data` table.

4. **Identify the criteria for 'errors'**:
   - 'Errors' are defined as those logged by the user in the last 7 days. The relevant column is `num_error_7d` in the `error_logs` table, and we need to ensure it is greater than 0.

5. **Formulate the SQL query**:
   - We will use an `INNER JOIN` to combine data from `users`, `user_metrics`, `session_data`, and `error_logs` tables based on the `user_id` column.
   - We will apply the necessary conditions in the `WHERE` clause to filter out users who meet all the criteria.
   - Since we need to check the average number of events (though the question specifies a condition on `total_events_7d` which is already an aggregate over 7 days, no need for further averaging here), and we need to ensure the average session time is above 5000 seconds.
   - Finally, we will select the `user_id` from the `users` table.

**Final SQL Query**:

```sql
SELECT 
  u.user_id
FROM 
  users u
  INNER JOIN user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
  INNER JOIN session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
  INNER JOIN error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
  u.user_churned = 0
  AND um.total_events_7d > 100
  AND sd.avg_time_per_session > 5000
  AND el.num_error_7d > 0;
```

This query efficiently retrieves the `user_id` of active users who have had more than 100 events, averaged over the last 7 days, spent more than 5000 seconds in an average session, and had at least one error in the last 7 days.
sql:
SELECT 
  u.user_id
FROM 
  users u
  INNER JOIN user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
  INNER JOIN session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
  INNER JOIN error_logs el ON u.user_id = el.user_id
WHERE 
  u.user_churned = 0
  AND um.total_events_7d > 100
  AND sd.avg_time_per_session > 5000
  AND el.num_error_7d > 0;

question: Find the user IDs of users with low engagement, defined as an overall engagement score of less than 5.0, and return them in order of their engagement score from highest to lowest.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to focus on the key requirements specified:

1. **Identify Users with Low Engagement**: Users with an overall engagement score of less than 5.0.
2. **Sort the Results**: Order the results by engagement score in descending order (highest to lowest).

Given these requirements, we can simplify the process by focusing only on the necessary tables and columns. The `user_engagement` table contains the `engagement_score` which is directly relevant to the question.

Here is the step-by-step reasoning:

1. **Select the `user_id` and `engagement_score` from the `user_engagement` table**: This table contains the engagement scores for each user, which is the primary metric for determining low engagement.
2. **Apply the Filter**: Only include users with an `engagement_score` of less than 5.0.
3. **Order the Results**: Sort the filtered results by `engagement_score` in descending order to see the highest scores first among the low-engagement users.

The resulting SQL query is:

```sql
SELECT 
    user_id
FROM 
    user_engagement
WHERE 
    engagement_score < 5.0
ORDER BY 
    engagement_score DESC;
```

This query efficiently retrieves the required user IDs based on the specified criteria and sorts them as requested.
sql:
SELECT 
    user_id
FROM 
    user_engagement
WHERE 
    engagement_score < 5.0
ORDER BY 
    engagement_score DESC;

question: Find the email addresses of users who have shown more than a 10% decrease in engagement over the last 30 days compared to the last 7 days, where the decrease is greater than 20%, and who have clicked on less than 5 ads in the last 7 days, and return the corresponding engagement decrease percentages.
cot:
To solve this problem, we need to identify users who meet the following criteria:

1. Users who have shown more than a 10% decrease in engagement over the last 30 days compared to the last 7 days.
2. The decrease in engagement should be greater than 20%.
3. These users should have clicked on less than 5 ads in the last 7 days.

We need to return the email addresses of these users along with the corresponding engagement decrease percentages.

Let's break down the steps to derive the SQL query:

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

1. **Identify Relevant Tables:**
   - `users`: To get the email addresses of the users.
   - `user_metrics`: To calculate the engagement decrease.
   - `ad_interactions`: To filter users based on the number of ad clicks.

2. **Join the Tables:**
   - Join `users` and `user_metrics` on `user_id` to get engagement metrics.
   - Join `user_metrics` and `ad_interactions` on `user_id` to filter users based on ad interactions.

3. **Calculate Engagement Decrease:**
   - The engagement decrease percentage is calculated using the formula:
     \[
     \text{engagement\_decrease\_percentage} = \left( \frac{\text{total\_events\_30d} - \text{total\_events\_7d}}{\text{total\_events\_30d}} \right) \times 100
     \]

4. **Apply Filters:**
   - Filter users where `total_events_30d` is greater than 0 to avoid division by zero.
   - Filter users where the engagement decrease percentage is greater than 20%.
   - Filter users where the number of ads clicked in the last 7 days is less than 5.

5. **Select Required Columns:**
   - Select `email` from the `users` table.
   - Select the calculated `engagement_decrease_percentage`.

6. **Final Query:**
   - Use `INNER JOIN` to combine the tables based on `user_id`.
   - Use `WHERE` to apply the necessary filters.
   - Use `SELECT` to specify the desired output columns.

### SQL Query

```sql
SELECT 
  u.email, 
  ((um.total_events_30d - um.total_events_7d) / um.total_events_30d) * 100 AS engagement_decrease_percentage
FROM 
  users u
INNER JOIN 
  user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
INNER JOIN 
  ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
WHERE 
  um.total_events_30d > 0 
  AND ((um.total_events_30d - um.total_events_7d) / um.total_events_30d) * 100 > 20
  AND ai.num_ads_clicked_7d < 5;
```

### Explanation:
- **Joins**: We join the `users`, `user_metrics`, and `ad_interactions` tables to gather all necessary data.
- **Filters**: We apply the conditions to ensure the users meet the specified criteria.
- **Calculation**: We calculate the engagement decrease percentage in the `SELECT` clause to include it in the output.

This query efficiently gathers the required information and filters the data to meet the problem's requirements.
sql:
SELECT 
  u.email, 
  ((um.total_events_30d - um.total_events_7d) / um.total_events_30d) * 100 AS engagement_decrease_percentage
FROM 
  users u
INNER JOIN 
  user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
INNER JOIN 
  ad_interactions ai ON u.user_id = ai.user_id
WHERE 
  um.total_events_30d > 0 
  AND ((um.total_events_30d - um.total_events_7d) / um.total_events_30d) * 100 > 20
  AND ai.num_ads_clicked_7d < 5;

question: Return the user IDs and their average engagement and ad experience scores for users with consistently high engagement and ad experience scores.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to understand the requirements and structure the query accordingly. Here's a step-by-step breakdown:

1. **Identify the Relevant Tables**: 
   The question asks for user IDs along with their average engagement and ad experience scores. Therefore, the `users`, `user_engagement`, and `ad_experience` tables are relevant.

2. **Understand the Conditions**:
   The question specifies "consistently high engagement and ad experience scores." This means we need to apply a threshold for both scores. The reference solution uses `e.engagement_score > 0.7` and `a.ad_experience_score > 0.6` in the `WHERE` clause to filter the records before they are grouped.

3. **Formulate the Aggregation**:
   Since we are interested in the average scores, we'll use the `AVG` function on `engagement_score` and `ad_experience_score`.

4. **Grouping**:
   Because we need the average scores per user, we will group the results by `user_id`.

5. **Final Filtering**:
   After grouping and calculating the averages, we need to ensure that the average scores are still above the specified thresholds. This is achieved using the `HAVING` clause.

6. **Construct the SQL Query**:
   Putting it all together, we construct the SQL query as follows:

```sql
SELECT 
  u.user_id, 
  AVG(e.engagement_score) AS avg_engagement_score, 
  AVG(a.ad_experience_score) AS avg_ad_experience_score
FROM 
  users u
  INNER JOIN user_engagement e ON u.user_id = e.user_id
  INNER JOIN ad_experience a ON u.user_id = a.user_id
WHERE 
  e.engagement_score > 0.7 AND a.ad_experience_score > 0.6
GROUP BY 
  u.user_id
HAVING 
  AVG(e.engagement_score) > 0.7 AND AVG(a.ad_experience_score) > 0.6;
```

### Detailed Reasoning:
- **Joins**: We use `INNER JOIN` to combine records from `users`, `user_engagement`, and `ad_experience` tables where the `user_id` matches in all tables.
- **Conditions in `WHERE` Clause**: These conditions filter out records that do not meet the threshold criteria before aggregation.
- **Aggregation and Grouping**: The `AVG` function calculates the average scores for each user, and `GROUP BY` groups the results by `user_id`.
- **Conditions in `HAVING` Clause**: These ensure that only those users with consistently high average scores are included in the final result set.

This query is efficient and directly addresses the question by focusing on users with consistently high engagement and ad experience scores.
sql:
SELECT 
  u.user_id, 
  AVG(e.engagement_score) AS avg_engagement_score, 
  AVG(a.ad_experience_score) AS avg_ad_experience_score
FROM 
  users u
  INNER JOIN user_engagement e ON u.user_id = e.user_id
  INNER JOIN ad_experience a ON u.user_id = a.user_id
WHERE 
  e.engagement_score > 0.7 AND a.ad_experience_score > 0.6
GROUP BY 
  u.user_id
HAVING 
  AVG(e.engagement_score) > 0.7 AND AVG(a.ad_experience_score) > 0.6;

question: Find the user IDs along with their 30-day moving average churn risk scores, ordered from highest to lowest risk, based on a risk score calculation that considers factors such as inactivity, engagement metrics, and user behavior.
cot:
To translate the natural language question into an executable SQLite query, we need to break down the requirements and map them to the appropriate calculations and data sources in the provided schema. Here is a step-by-step approach:

1. **Identify Relevant Data Sources**: We need to gather data from multiple tables to calculate the churn risk score. Key factors include inactivity, engagement metrics, and user behavior.

2. **Define Churn Risk Calculation**: We need to define a churn risk score based on the given factors such as:
   - Days since active.
   - Repeats ratio.
   - Total events in the last 7 days.
   - Average time per session.
   - Number of ads clicked in the last 7 days.
   - Number of thumbs down in the last 7 days.
   - Profile completeness.
   - Number of playlists created.
   - Number of upgrades and downgrades.
   - Number of errors in the last 7 days.

3. **Calculate Moving Average**: We need to compute a 30-day moving average of the churn risk scores.

4. **Order Results**: Finally, we need to order the results from highest to lowest risk.

### Step-by-Step Solution

1. **Create a Common Table Expression (CTE) to Gather All Relevant Data**:
   - Join the `users` table with other tables to collect all necessary metrics.
   
2. **Calculate Churn Risk Score**:
   - Define a churn risk score based on the specified factors using a `CASE` statement.
   
3. **Compute 30-Day Moving Average**:
   - Use the `AVG()` function with an `OVER` clause to calculate the moving average.

4. **Select and Order the Results**:
   - Select the user IDs and their moving average churn risk scores.
   - Order the results in descending order of the moving average churn risk.

Here is the resulting SQL query:

```sql
WITH UserEngagementCTE AS (
    SELECT
        u.user_id,
        u.days_since_active,
        u.repeats_ratio,
        um.total_events_7d,
        sd.avg_time_per_session,
        ad.num_ads_clicked_7d,
        tf.num_thumbs_down_7d,
        pf.profile_completeness,
        pl.num_playlists_created,
        sh.num_upgrades,
        sh.num_downgrades,
        er.num_error_7d
    FROM
        users u
    LEFT JOIN
        user_metrics um ON u.user_id = um.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        session_data sd ON u.user_id = sd.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        ad_interactions ad ON u.user_id = ad.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        thumbs_feedback tf ON u.user_id = tf.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        user_profiles pf ON u.user_id = pf.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        playlist_actions pl ON u.user_id = pl.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        subscription_history sh ON u.user_id = sh.user_id
    LEFT JOIN
        error_logs er ON u.user_id = er.user_id
),
ChurnRiskScoreCTE AS (
    SELECT
        ue.user_id,
        (CASE
            WHEN ue.days_since_active > 30 THEN 10
            WHEN ue.repeats_ratio < 0.5 THEN 5
            WHEN ue.total_events_7d < 10 THEN 5
            WHEN ue.avg_time_per_session < 60 THEN 5
            WHEN ue.num_ads_clicked_7d < 1 THEN 5
            WHEN ue.num_thumbs_down_7d > 2 THEN 5
            WHEN ue.profile_completeness < 50 THEN 5
            ELSE 0
        END) AS churn_risk_score
    FROM
        UserEngagementCTE ue
),
FinalChurnAnalysis AS (
    SELECT
        cr.user_id,
        cr.churn_risk_score,
        AVG(cr.churn_risk_score) OVER (ORDER BY cr.user_id ROWS BETWEEN 30 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_churn_risk
    FROM
        ChurnRiskScoreCTE cr
)
SELECT
    fca.user_id,
    fca.moving_avg_churn_risk
FROM
    FinalChurnAnalysis fca
ORDER BY
    fca.moving_avg_churn_risk DESC;
```

### Explanation:
- **UserEngagementCTE**: This CTE gathers all relevant data from the users and their associated tables.
- **ChurnRiskScoreCTE**: This CTE calculates the churn risk score for each user based on the defined criteria.
- **FinalChurnAnalysis**: This CTE calculates the 30-day moving average of the churn risk score for each user.
- **Final SELECT**: The final query selects the user IDs and their 30-day moving average churn risk scores, ordering them from highest to lowest risk.

question: Find the inactive users who are 'sleeping beauties', along with their demographic information, subscription details, session metrics, ad interaction metrics, engagement metrics, and subscription plan changes, ranked by how long they've been dormant and their level of engagement, to identify who is most at risk of being 'poisoned apples' and abandoning the service.
external_knowledge: Inactive users are 'sleeping beauties' who have been inactive for more than 30 days; 'poisoned apples' refer to users who are at high risk of churning; 'dormant' refers to the period of inactivity as measured by the 'days_since_active' column.
cot:
To address the natural language question, we need to identify users who are 'sleeping beauties'—users inactive for more than 30 days—and gather their demographic information, subscription details, session metrics, ad interaction metrics, engagement metrics, and subscription plan changes. We will rank these users based on the duration of their inactivity and their level of engagement to determine those most at risk of churning.

Let's break down the steps required to build the SQL query:

1. **Identify Inactive Users**: Select users who have been inactive for more than 30 days based on the `days_since_active` column.

2. **Retrieve Demographic Information**: Fetch relevant demographic details from the `users` table such as `gender`, `age`, `location`.

3. **Fetch Subscription Details**: Obtain details about the users' subscription plan and their churn status from the `users` table.

4. **Gather Session Metrics**: Collect session-related metrics from the `session_data` table, including the average number of sessions and average session duration.

5. **Obtain Ad Interaction Metrics**: Extract ad interaction data from the `ad_interactions` table, such as the number of ads clicked and skipped in the last 7 days.

6. **Retrieve Engagement Metrics**: Get user engagement scores and trends from the `user_engagement` table.

7. **Get Subscription Plan Changes**: Collect data on the number of plan changes and the current plan from the `subscription_history` table.

8. **Rank Users**: Order the results by `days_since_active` in descending order (to prioritize longer periods of inactivity) and `engagement_score` in ascending order (to prioritize users with lower engagement).

Now, let's construct the SQL query based on these steps:

```sql
WITH InactiveUsers AS (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        days_since_active,
        subscription_plan,
        user_churned,
        gender,
        age,
        location
    FROM users
    WHERE days_since_active > 30
),
UserSessionMetrics AS (
    SELECT 
        sd.user_id,
        AVG(sd.num_sessions) AS avg_sessions,
        AVG(sd.avg_time_per_session) AS avg_session_duration
    FROM session_data sd
    JOIN InactiveUsers iu ON sd.user_id = iu.user_id
    GROUP BY sd.user_id
),
AdInteractionMetrics AS (
    SELECT 
        ai.user_id,
        ai.num_ads_clicked_7d,
        ai.num_ads_skipped_7d
    FROM ad_interactions ai
    JOIN InactiveUsers iu ON ai.user_id = iu.user_id
),
UserEngagementMetrics AS (
    SELECT 
        ue.user_id,
        ue.engagement_score,
        ue.engagement_trend
    FROM user_engagement ue
    JOIN InactiveUsers iu ON ue.user_id = iu.user_id
),
SubscriptionPlanChanges AS (
    SELECT 
        sh.user_id,
        sh.total_plan_changes,
        sh.current_plan
    FROM subscription_history sh
    JOIN InactiveUsers iu ON sh.user_id = iu.user_id
)
SELECT 
    iu.user_id,
    iu.gender,
    iu.age,
    iu.location,
    iu.subscription_plan,
    iu.days_since_active,
    usm.avg_sessions,
    usm.avg_session_duration,
    aim.num_ads_clicked_7d,
    aim.num_ads_skipped_7d,
    uem.engagement_score,
    uem.engagement_trend,
    spc.total_plan_changes,
    spc.current_plan,
    iu.user_churned
FROM InactiveUsers iu
LEFT JOIN UserSessionMetrics usm ON iu.user_id = usm.user_id
LEFT JOIN AdInteractionMetrics aim ON iu.user_id = aim.user_id
LEFT JOIN UserEngagementMetrics uem ON iu.user_id = uem.user_id
LEFT JOIN SubscriptionPlanChanges spc ON iu.user_id = spc.user_id
ORDER BY iu.days_since_active DESC, uem.engagement_score ASC;
```

### Explanation:
- **InactiveUsers CTE**: Filters users who have been inactive for over 30 days.
- **UserSessionMetrics CTE**: Averages session metrics over all sessions for each inactive user.
- **AdInteractionMetrics CTE**: Collects recent ad interaction metrics.
- **UserEngagementMetrics CTE**: Retrieves user engagement scores and trends.
- **SubscriptionPlanChanges CTE**: Gathers details on subscription history changes for inactive users.
- **Final SELECT Statement**: Joins all CTEs to gather comprehensive information about inactive users and orders them by inactivity duration and engagement score to identify potential 'poisoned apples'.

This query ensures that we have all necessary data for each user and that the results are ranked appropriately to highlight users at high risk of churning.
